name: Days of Inventory Cover
description: How many days current stock will last at the average usage rate: available stock divided by average daily demand.
assumptions: Average daily demand is representative of the coming period.
Stock and demand are expressed in the same unit (units, cases, or value).
limitations: A crude single-item view; it ignores demand variability and in-transit stock.
For financial reporting the reciprocal (stockturn) is often used instead; weeks of cover = stock value / annual usage x 52.

examples.note: Illustrative application of the cited stock-cover definition: 600 units on hand at 100 units/day lasts 6 days.

citation.quote: the time in which the stock will run out at average usage rate.

sources: Best Practice in Inventory Management (stock cover); Supply Chain Manager's Handbook (days of supply)

citations.quote: Overall stock cover is calculated from the total value of stock divided by the annual issue value and multiplied by 52 to give data in terms of weeks.
Days of supply ... uses average daily consumption based on historical demand to calculate an order quantity for a defined number of days of supply.
